title-group.article-title: Patterns, drivers, and trends of urban cooling demand across global cities

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Urban cooling demand (UCD) is shaped by the three-dimensional evolution of cities, where climate, morphology, and demographic pressures interact to determine thermal energy needs. However, urbanization is driving heterogeneous volumetric growth, spanning horizontal expansion, vertical densification, and infilling, whose combined effects on UCD remain poorly quantified. Here, we analyse 88 Indian and 52 global cities from 2003 to 2023 and reveal a divergence in UCD trajectories driven by growth typologies. Cities undergoing extensive, irregular expansion exhibit the fastest relative increases in UCD despite lower initial demand, whereas medium-sized cities sustain the highest absolute demand. Compact urban forms show moderated trends, indicating structural thermal advantages. Across regions, volumetric growth, particularly vertical development and volumetric densification, is strongly associated with UCD and exhibits substantial explanatory power after climate, suggesting that urban form is an important consideration in climate-responsive and energy-efficient urban transitions.

sec.p: Subject terms: Environmental impact, Environmental health

abstract.p: The authors show that city growth patterns strongly shape future cooling demand, with sprawling and vertically densifying cities showing the fastest rise, highlighting critical risks for energy demand, heat exposure, and climate-resilient urban planning.

sec.p: Received 2025 Jul 5; Accepted 2026 May 26; Collection date 2026.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cities are considered as fundamental units of climate change adaptation and mitigation1. Urbanization-induced development of cities drives multi-scale environmental change2, including global warming3. The physical expansion of urban built forms, growth pattern of cities, and the associated CO2 emissions from this development collectively drive local and regional warming, compounding the rise in global temperatures1,4,5. As urban areas continue to warm, city-wide annual indoor cooling needs, henceforth called urban cooling demand (UCD), are expected to rise6. However, beyond the rate or scale of urbanization, the impact of different spatial development patterns on UCD at city scale remains underexplored. While recent Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) assessment reports highlight the role of urban structure, form, and the relationships between physical elements, spaces, and activities in shaping urban energy use3, there is limited quantitative understanding of their collective influence on cooling demand.

The evolution of cities and their built form is shaped by a complex interplay of socio-economic and environmental factors7. The scope of our study is limited to the physical evolution of cities and built-forms. Urban built forms evolve through three key processes that reshape city volume: (i) lateral or horizontal expansion, where the urban fringe is developed into engineered landscapes8; (ii) structural development, which includes vertical growth, as taller buildings replace shorter ones9, and infilling10 which occurs via a transition from open spaces to built infrastructure; (iii) and demographic densification, where changes in population density influence the extent and form of growth8. Collectively and through time, these changes shape urban volumetric changes. The resulting processes, henceforth called volumetric growth, define patterns of urban growth, which characterize the structure and function of cities11. These processes also collectively impact surface energy dynamics12–14, creating temperature differentials within and around urban areas15. In hot and humid as well as semi-arid regions, such temperature variations adversely affect urban quality of life by causing fluctuations in cooling energy demand16 and can pose significant health risks17. Moreover, these concerns are compounded by socio-economic disparities, resulting in a disproportionate heat burden on city dwellers18. Despite over 13,000 cities worldwide experiencing such temperature variations and extreme heat stress19,20 there is limited scientific understanding and empirical evidence on how different components of city-volumetric growth interact to shape UCD. Addressing this knowledge gap is essential for developing effective adaptation and mitigation strategies tailored to the diverse urban growth trajectories observed globally.

Remote sensing and numerical modelling studies focused on lateral expansion of cities, attributing 20–50% of warming and up to 75% of cooling demand to urbanization21–24. Spatial density of built forms is identified as a driver of cooling demand24. However, studies rarely provide an integrated understanding of UCD variations across multiple cities, with particular focus on the relative roles of city size (lateral and vertical extent both), built-ground coverage (indicator of compactness as well as imperviousness), and population density growth together. Recently, Zhou et al.25 Frolking et al.11 highlighted vertical development as a critical yet overlooked factor in urbanization assessment studies that necessitate further study to ascertain their contribution to UCD. Numerical models often consider urban parameters: such as lateral expansion (CH), vertical development (CV) which shape urban canyon depths, built-ground coverage reflecting compactness as well as the share of impervious cover in urban cores (CIR), and population density (CP) in a simplified form or based on Local Climate Zone classifications, which do not adequately capture the actual volumetric growth dynamics of urban areas26. While there are recent attempts to capture the detailed impact of CP,CH,CIR on heat and cold exposure studies27, integrating observation-based high-resolution actual volumetric data increases computational complexity28, limiting large-scale assessments and leading to inconsistencies in intra-city UCD analysis29–31. Addressing these gaps is crucial for accurately quantifying city-scale structural growth impact on UCD.

In this study, we quantitatively examine the relationship between UCD and key indicators of city-scale volumetric growth (Supplementary Fig. 1) for 88 Indian and 52 globally prominent cities. Leveraging high-resolution satellite and reanalysis datasets from 2003–2023, harmonized to a common spatial resolution of ~1 km and spatially aggregated within urban boundaries, we incorporate indicators such as varied measures of vertical expansion, lateral urban spread, infill densification, population density, vegetation cover, and road network intensity; dimensions that collectively characterize the evolving urban form. Our study advances and complements prior works in two key aspects, which we test in form of two hypotheses: (1) that the trajectory of volumetric growth, shaped by geomorphological factors and historical contingencies surrounding the growth of cities, influences UCD; and (2) that vertical expansion and volumetric densification have a stronger impact on UCD than horizontal expansion, particularly in rapidly developing cities coming under lower-middle income as well as high and upper-middle income classification, following the World Bank income classification of countries32. We classify these cities into four typologies based on shared volumetric growth characteristics, with additional stratifications based on regional and climatic factors. Using a humidity-weighted Cooling Degree Days (CDD-hum) approach as a proxy for UCD, derived using reanalysis meteorological data (ERA5-and), we capture monthly-to-annual demand dynamics with high temporal coherence (R ≈ 0.7–0.9, p < 0.05) (see methods). This approach proves especially useful for Indian cities, where fine-resolution cooling energy data remain sparse. Our findings demonstrate that typology-specific growth patterns, when analysed alongside climatic and morphological context, exert a measurable influence on UCD trajectories. Prior global studies have already identified India, alongside Indonesia and China, as regions with rising UCD33. With nearly 8% of Indian households currently equipped with air conditioning (AC), projections indicate a six-fold increase in ACs over the next two decades34,35. The methodological framework and findings present here are thus not only critical for understanding the drivers of UCD in Indian cities, but also offer a broader lens to compare urban cooling demand trajectories across cities of Global South with lower-middle income, providing a foundation for future regional and global assessments of climate-responsive urbanization.

sec.title: Patterns of annual UCD and its trends
sec.p: Our analysis of UCD across 2003 to 2023 shows that approximately 90% of analyzed Indian cities, situated in hot-humid, composite and semi-arid regions, such as Kolkata, Delhi, Chennai, and Jaipur, experience a substantial cooling demand, with mean CDD-hum of 1963 ± 78.46 °C-year (mean ± standard error). This translates to an average of 273 ± 1 days requiring cooling, with 22 °C base temperature. When the base temperature is increased to 28 °C, the CDD-hum decreases by 58% (821.86 ± 38 °C-year), with a corresponding reduction in cooling-demand days to 146 ± 5 (Fig. 1 and Supplementary Figs. 20–22). Cities situated in cold climatic zones, primarily in the southern, western Himalayan foothills including Srinagar and Imphal, exhibit markedly lower cooling demands, with CDD-hum ranging from 82 to 438 °C-years and only 50 to 150 warmer days annually. Regionally, eastern coastal cities exhibit the highest demand (2514 ± 224 °C-year, with 22°C base temperature), exceeding western coastal and inland regions, despite similar climate classifications (Fig. 1 and Supplementary Table 3). The estimated CDD-hum across 52 global cities also showed an overall increase of 1.12 ± 0.12% in cooling demand over the past two decades (Fig. 2). However, the mean CDD-hum value remained significantly lower (~741.82 ± 99 °C-years) compared to that observed in Indian cities. Days requiring cooling generally fell within the range of 120 or more, as unlike Indian cities, many western cities also have a substantial reliance on space heating. Similar to Indian cities, the major coastal and inland cities across Asia did not exhibit significant differences in UCD. In contrast, pronounced regional variations were observed in the United States and Europe. Specifically, US inland cities exhibited significantly higher UCD values compared to their coastal counterparts, whereas in Europe, coastal cities showed relatively higher UCD than inland cities (Table 2 and Fig. 2).

caption.title: Patterns and trends of UCD across 88 Indian cities over the past two decades, modelled using humidity-weighted Cooling Degree Days (CDD-hum) with a base temperature of 22–28 °C.
caption.p: a Shows the average CDD-hum (°C-year) over the period 2003–2023, while b and c illustrate the linear (°C-year/year) and log-linear trends (%/year) of CDD-hum, respectively, based on ordinary least squares regression. Bubble size represents the relative magnitude of values across cities. Bubble edge colour represents volumetric city typology in panels a–c and regional setting in panels d–f. Boxplots in the first three panels (a–c) of the first row represent the distribution of trends across different volumetric city typologies (Type-I to Type-IV), while those in the last three panels (d–f) represent the distribution across regional settings of cities. Boxplots summarize distributions across volumetric typologies (Type I–IV) in a–c and across regional settings in d–f. Boxplots indicate the median (centre line), interquartile range (IQR; 25th–75th percentiles), whiskers extending to 1.5×IQR, and points beyond whiskers as outliers. d–f replicate panels a–c, respectively, with colour coding representing regional groupings: East Coast (EC), West Coast (WC), Indo-Gangetic Plain (IG), South and Western Himalayan Foothills and the North-Eastern Hill Region (SH), Interior Peninsular/Deccan Plateau (DP), and Central and Western India (CI). The administrative boundary of India used in this study was obtained from the Survey of India, Online maps Portal142.

caption.title: Patterns and trends of UCD across 52 global cities (excluding Indian cities) over the past two decades, modelled using humidity-weighted Cooling Degree Days (CDD-hum) with a base temperature of 20 °C.
caption.p: a Shows the average CDD-hum (°C-year), while b and c show the linear (°C-year/year) and log-linear (%/year) trends, respectively based on ordinary least squares regression. Bubble size represents the relative magnitude across cities, and edge colour represents volumetric city typology. First column of boxplots in a–c represents continental distributions (Asia (ASIA), Australia (AUS), Europe (EUR), and United States (US)); while corresponding second column also show volumetric typology-based distributions (Type-I to Type-IV). Boxplots indicate the median (centre line), interquartile range (IQR; 25th–75th percentiles), whiskers extending to 1.5×IQR, and points beyond whiskers as outliers. The administrative boundary of global countries used in this study was obtained from the World Bank Official Boundaries portal32.
The estimated values of CDD-hum across the other Global cities and their rising trends per annum in the time period 2003–2023

CDD-hum overall trend (°C-year) in simple linear regression (20 °C)
CDD-hum overall trend (%) in Log-linear regression (20 °C)

fn.p: The values are shown as mean ± standard error format. The cities are grouped into four typologies based on their volumetric characteristics.

sec.p: The group of type-I and type-II cities together, characterized by high population densities and extensive to medium volumetric expansion, exhibit the higher CDD-hum values and exceedance durations (Table 1 and Fig. 1). Type-II cities, which undergo rapid vertical densification alongside continuous horizontal expansion at a faster pace than type-I cities, in India, display the highest cooling energy demands. Interestingly, despite their substantial built-up volumes, type-I cities exhibit CDD-hum levels comparable to smaller cities (type-III, type-IV). Among cities outside India, Type-I cities exhibited substantially higher UCD as well as higher warming days, followed by Type-II cities, while Type-III and Type-IV cities recorded nearly half the demand observed in Type-I cities. Although the number of global cities outside India included in the analysis is relatively limited, making it difficult to draw definitive conclusions, the markedly greater lateral spread observed in Type-I global cities (Supplementary Figs. 20, 21) appears to be a key contributing factor to their elevated UCD levels, in addition to the influence of background climate conditions (Supplementary Table 4 and Fig. 2).

caption.p: The estimated values of CDD-hum across the Indian cities and their rising trends per annum within the time period 2003–2023

fn.p: The values are shown as mean ± standard error format. The cities are grouped into four typologies based on their volumetric characteristics.

sec.p: Analysis of trends of rise in CDD-hum for Indian cities showed a consistent upward trend, with an average annual increase, under the assumption of a linear increase, of 6.4 ± 0.9 °C-year (mean ± standard error). Considering the background climate and regional settings, Indian cities exhibited distinct trends in the rise of CDD-hum (Table 1 and Fig. 1). Cities located in the foothills of the southern and western Himalayas exhibited the highest relative rise in CDD-hum, followed by those on the east coast and then the west coast. Cities situated in the Indo-Gangetic Plain and the Deccan Plateau showed moderate increases, both in terms of absolute rise and log-linear relative-trends. While absolute increases reflect the total growth in cooling demand, relative increases capture proportional changes, enabling meaningful comparisons across regions with differing baseline conditions. For global cities, the Australian cities showed comparable trends in the relative rise in CDD-hum to Indian cities (~0.2–0.3% per annum). Cities experiencing warm and humid conditions (typically concentrated in southern Asian coastal as well as inland regions) showed similar trends of CDD-hum rise as Indian cities facing comparable climatic conditions (~0.4–0.7%). European cities showed the highest rise in CDD-hum (~2.5–5%), primarily due to the rapid impact of global warming in last two decades on temperate regions across European countries36. However, in absolute terms, the values are around 5 °C-year, significantly lower than those observed in the US and Asia (~5–10 °C-year) (Supplementary Table 4 and Fig. 2).

Volumetric classification-specific trends in CDD-hum indicate distinct cooling demand rise patterns. In Indian cities under study, type-I cities exhibit the highest average rate of increase, at 3.4 ± 1 to 11.5 ± 6 °C-year per annum, followed by Type-II (~5.8 ± 1.1 °C-year/year). Type-III and type-IV cities display comparable trends, with type-III cities showing less variability around the mean compared to type-IV cities (Fig. 1, Supplementary Figs. 20–22 and Table 1). The log-linear trend for the increase in CDD-hum across all the cities showed a rise of ~3.5% per decade, except type-I cities (~6% per decade). For global cities, outside India, the trend in absolute increase of CDD-hum is highest for type-I, II (~6 ± 1 °C-year per annum) cities followed by type-III and type-IV (~3-4 °C-year per annum). The majority of inland Asian cities, which primarily fall under the Type-I classification, also showed a comparable rise in UCD to Type-I cities in India. While the patterns of CDD-hum rise in absolute terms for global cities outside India and Indian cities are similar, global cities (~0.9–1.5%) exhibit a significantly higher relative increase in CDD-hum compared to Indian cities across all typologies (~0.2–0.5%) (Fig. 2 and Table 2).

sec.title: Bivariate and semi-partial correlation of built-urbanization
sec.p: Indicators of volumetric growth of cities, representing urbanization trends, showed statistically significant correlation with the city-wide rising trends of CDD-hum as well as the 20-year average of annual CDD-hum (henceforth as avg. CDD-hum). Using both Pearson correlation coefficients and Spearman rank correlations, we assessed the linear and monotonic (non-linear) relationships (with significance level p < 0.1 and p < 0.05) between actual and relative increases in CDD-hum, avg. CDD-hum and various indicators of city volumetric growth (Fig. 3 and see Supplementary Figs. 23–31).

caption.title: Bivariate Pearson correlations between urban volumetric indicators and rising CDD-hum trends across Indian cities.
caption.p: a Correlations with the absolute increase in CDD-hum; b correlations with the relative increase. Statistical significance is denoted as p < 0.1 (*) and p < 0.05 (**), based on two-sided tests. Colours represent the four volumetric typologies (Type-I to Type-IV), and the grey bars indicate overall correlations across all cities. Y-axis labels denote urban volumetric indicators grouped under broader dimensions of urban structural change: lateral extent (LAT. EXT.), population (POP.), infilling (INFILL), vertical expansion (VER. EXT.), volumetric growth (VOL. GROW.), volumetric densification (DENSIFIC.), consolidation, green cover (GREEN), land surface temperature (LST), anthropogenic heat (AH), and GDP. Detailed urban variable definitions are provided in Supplementary Table 5. Correlations are computed after excluding values beyond 1.5×IQR for each variable to limit the influence of extreme observations on Pearson estimates; corresponding full-dataset results are shown in Supplementary Fig. 23 and yield consistent pattern.

sec.p: Cities expanding horizontally - lateral expanse of built-up exhibit a positive linear correlation with rising CDD-hum trends (R ~ 0.–0.8) for Indian cities (Fig. 3 and Supplementary Fig. 23). The annual increase in lateral extent of the built environment (CH↑) and related metrics further reinforce this relationship, highlighting the role of urban sprawl and population-rise driven lateral expansion of cities in exacerbating cooling demand across all city types over time. These effects are most pronounced in both Indian as well as Global Type-I and Type-II cities (Supplementary Figs. 23–28). In contrast, smaller cities (Type-III and IV together) show weaker associations, reflecting slower lateral expansion and reduced contributions from the increase in lateral extent. Interestingly, Type-IV cities, characterized by compact urban development, indicated a weak negative correlation between (CH↑) and CDD-hum trends when analysed separately. These cities also exhibit a weak negative correlation between CDD-hum trends and log(CP), further supporting the idea that small, compact cities undergoing moderate expansion may reflect a temporary reduction in urban density-related thermal effects. This suggests that within certain limits, lateral growth in Type-IV cities could initially help mitigate cooling demand by balanced lateral and vertical compactness. However, this effect likely diminishes as expansion progresses, eventually leading to heat accumulation, beyond a critical threshold. The lateral expansion indicators also showed a moderate, nonlinear, and positive association with the rise in CDD-hum, with the effect most pronounced in megacities across both lower-middle income as well as high and upper-middle income regions under study (Supplementary Figs. 26–28).

City Infilling, greenery reduction, road network in tandem with lateral expansion - Infilling as well as the measure of imperviousness exhibit a moderate positive correlation with the rising trends of CDD-hum. The rate of average increment in urban infilling across the lateral expanse of cities, represented by (CIR*CH↑) showed a stronger positive correlation (R ~ 0.3-0.7, p < 0.1) in both linear as well as in nonlinear association measures (Fig. 3 and Supplementary Fig. 23). These observations are most pronounced in Type-I and Type-II cities, while for Type-III, and IV cities, such a relationship was not apparent when metrics were correlated separately. For Indian cities, urban consolidation around city cores, defined as the city lateral expanse combined with logarithm of population density, infilling in cities, consistently exhibit the strong positive correlations with the annual increase in CDD-hum (R > 0.45), highlighting the role of overall urban infilling and population driven built form development, increasing the cooling demand requirement (Fig. 3, Supplementary Fig. 23–28).

The relative densification of urban patches expressed in terms of (increase in CH per capita) demonstrated negative correlation with CDD-hum rise across the globe (R ~ −0.25 to −0.65). The rise of green cover showed a moderate negative correlation, with the effect most pronounced in Type-I and Type-II cities (R ~ –0.2 to –0.45), indicating that a reduction in greenery and densification of lands contributes to the rise in UCD in overall. This impact was evident in both linear and nonlinear association analyses. Furthermore, indices measuring road network characteristics across cities showed a moderate to strong positive linear association with the rise in CDD-hum (R ~ 0.4–0.6). Expansion of road networks contributes to increased impervious surface coverage, making it a potential city-scale driver of the observed CDD-hum trends (Fig. 3; Supplementary Figs. 23–28).

Cities expanding vertically - When analysing the vertical rise of cities, data is often organized into percentiles to understand the distribution of building height thresholds, rather than taking averages within the city-lateral extent25. The lowest 25% of building heights fall into the CVN(0−25) percentile, representing areas with the shortest buildings, such as residential or commercial neighborhood, or older parts of the city. From the 25th−50th percentile, categorized as CVN(25−50), includes mid-rise buildings like larger residential complexes or smaller office buildings. The CVN50−75, percentile encompasses the middle to upper range of building heights, featuring taller office buildings, high-rise residential complexes, and significant commercial developments. Finally, the highest 25% of building heights are represented by CVN(75−100), and include the tallest skyscrapers, major commercial centers, and high-density urban developments. The vertical rise in cities across all the major percentile distributions (CVN(25−100)) showed moderate to strong positive correlations (R ~ 0.2-0.6) with relative increase of CDD-hum (Fig. 3; Supplementary Figs. 23–28), with this trend evident for Indian as well as Global Type-I cities. Further, our analysis across global and Indian cities shows a negative linear correlation between the quartile coefficient of dispersion (QCD) and rising CDD-hum trends (R ≈ –0.36 ). Physically, the QCD reflects the variability in building heights across a city, with higher values indicating a more uneven vertical profile variability within a city. Our observation here, suggests that greater vertical heterogeneity is associated with reduced cooling demand, but not for all the cities, specifically this effect is most prominent in smaller, compact Indian cities. For instance, a city with uniform building heights tends to show a steeper increase in CDD-hum compared to one with a more varied high rise, implying that diverse vertical form may be associated with reduced increase in temperature-related cooling demand within the urban extent of such cities.

To observe the trends of possible vertical growth associated with horizontal urban expansion and infilling as well as increases in population density, the measures (e.g., CVN(25−75,100)*(CH↑), CV(25−75,100)*(CP↑),(CIR*CVN(25−75,100)) is examined. Initially urban population densities were used for 3D urban representation37. By combining the vertical rise of cities with horizontal expansion and infilling, as well as changes in population density, these metrics indirectly assess how vertical growth complements lateral densification in both built and demographic terms. This approach allows for an indirect understanding of how cities balance vertical development with horizontal expansion, providing insights into the dynamics of urban densification. Our results indicate moderate to strong correlation (R > 0.45–0.75, p < 0.1) (Fig. 3; Supplementary Figs. 23–31), suggesting that increased building heights across cities contribute to increased cooling demand, likely due to altered airflow, heat retention, and shifts in the surface energy balance of urban facets. This impact is again both valid in linear and nonlinear association measure, making this metric as one of the possible determinants of UCD variations in cities in association with metrics such as CH↑ or CP↑ or compactness.

Volumetric expansion of cities tandem with population-driven densification - The gross volumetric measure of cities, estimated by city lateral expansion multiplied by inter-quartile height distribution (CV(25−75,100)*CH) shows a strong positive correlation (R ~ 0.7) with rising trend of CDD-hum, both in absolute as well as relative terms. For global cities under study, the impact is diminished. The overall growth rate of city volume in terms of CV(25−75,100)*(CH↑) also resulted in a similarly strong positive correlation across the entire set of cities (R ~ 0.6). These effects are particularly pronounced in larger cities, where expansive urban form and vertical growth contribute significantly to rising UCD, as also noted from last discussions (Fig. 3; Supplementary Figs. 13–19). The volumetric densification of cities defined in terms of CV(25−75,100)*(CP↑) or (CP↑). showed a contrasting relation with avg. CDD-hum for Indian cities and Global cities. In case of Indian cities, the observed correlation is moderately negative (R ~ −0.2–0.5) whereas for Global cities its moderate to positively strong (R ~ 0.6) (Supplementary Figs. 29–31).

Semi-partial correlation controlled for city-lateral expansion and population density - The semi-partial correlation of built-urbanization indicators, while controlling for urban lateral expansion and population density, is shown in Fig. 4 for Indian cities and in Fig. 5 for global cities. The horizontal axis (Bivariate Correlation) represents the direct correlation between each indicator and the trends of CDD-hum. The vertical axis (Semi-Partial Correlation) shows the additional explanatory power of each indicator after accounting for urban lateral expansion and population density. This highlights the strength of the linear correlation between CDD-hum trends and the remaining urbanization indicators, particularly city vertical growth. Several indicators provide additional explanations over city size and population density alone. Among these, the annual rise in built area multiplied by the IQR of vertical expanse of cities, CV(25−75,100or0−50)*(CHorCH↑) exhibits the strongest semi-partial correlation, suggesting its potential as the second most influential positive determinant in CDD-hum trends in Indian cities. Interestingly the relative densification or consolidations in Indian cities expressed in terms of CIR*CH, CIR*CV(25−75,100or0−50) or, CV(25−75,100or0−50)*(CP↑) and green coverage can act as moderate attributors of CDD-hum rise reduction. In case of Global cities (excluding Indian cities) city vertical rise CV(25−75,100or0−50) is the strongest attributor in CDD-Hum rise where as, CV(25−75,100or0−50)*(CP↑), CIR*CV(25−75,100or0−50) act as second most important positive attributor. In terms of strong impacting factor, the city infilling in combination with log of population density (CIR*log(CP)) can be potential choice for these cities (Fig. 5 and Supplementary Figs. 32–35).

caption.title: The semi-partial correlation between rising trends of CDD-hum for Indian cities, controlling for city area and population density.
caption.p: a, b Shows the Semi-partial Pearson correlations with absolute and relative CDD-hum trends respectively, controlling for city area and population density. c, d Shows the semi-partial Spearman rank correlations, controlling for the same variables and for same target variables. The vertical axis shows the semi-partial correlation coefficient, while the horizontal axis shows the corresponding bivariate (uncontrolled) correlation. Statistical significance is denoted as p < 0.1 (*) and p < 0.05 (**), based on two-sided tests. Grouped y-axis labels represent broader dimensions of urban structural change, including lateral extent (LAT.), population (POP.), infilling (INFIL.), vertical expansion (VER.), volumetric growth (VOL. GROW.), densification (DENSIFIC.), consolidation (COND.), green cover (GREEN), land surface temperature (LST), anthropogenic heat (AH), and economic activity (GDP). Detailed features and their definitions under each broad categorization are provided in Supplementary Table 5.

caption.p: a, b Shows the Semi-partial Pearson correlations with absolute and relative CDD-hum trends respectively, controlling for city area and population density. c, d Shows the semi-partial Spearman rank correlations, controlling for the same variables and for same target variables. The vertical axis shows the semi-partial correlation coefficient, while the horizontal axis shows the corresponding bivariate (uncontrolled) correlation. Statistical significance is denoted as p < 0.1 (*) and p < 0.05 (**), based on two-sided tests. Grouped y-axis labels represent broader dimensions of urban structural change, including lateral extent (LAT.), population (POP.), infilling (INFIL.), vertical expansion (VER.), volumetric growth (VOL. GROW.), densification (DENSIFIC.), consolidation (COND.), green cover (GREEN), land surface temperature (LST), anthropogenic heat (AH), and economic activity (GDP). Detailed urban features and their definitions under each broad categorization are provided in Supplementary Table 5.

sec.title: Modelling attempt for cross-city UCD trends
sec.p: Linear regression models including Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO), Elastic Net, and Bayesian Ridge regression methods38,39 were employed to identify interpretable indicators of city-scale urbanization which can be potential predictors for cross city UCD estimation (see Table 3, model cluster 1,3,5,7). Further, methods such as Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)40 were also implemented to evaluate the dynamic non-linear relationship between urbanization indicators and cross-city variability in UCD (model cluster 2,4,6,8). The cross-country variability in UCD in the models were accessed using two separate target variables: two-decade average of annual UCD (model 1,2 and 3,4) and annual rise in UCD (model 5,6 and 7,8), given same set of input feature spaces. When the rising trend of CDD-hum was used as the target variable, the models exhibited limited generalizability (R² ~0.4–0.7, CV MSPE ~ 0.94–1.5) (see Table 3). Indicators of city vertical expanse, along with indicators of volumetric growth, densification, and green cover reduction, showed a relatively modest but noticeable influence alongside climatic and contextual impact. In contrast, when average CDD-hum was used as the target variable, the models performed substantially better (R² ~ 0.8–0.9, CV MSPE ~ 0.05–0.13). Across all the models the influence of urbanization was less dominant than that of warming days, which largely reflect the combined effects of climate and regional setting across all the models (see Fig. 6 and Supplementary Fig. 36). Here in models, targeting average CDD-hum, urbanization indicators related to volumetric expansion, height distribution measures, retained substantial importance, suggesting that the three-dimensional characteristics of urban form may be the second most important predictors of UCD variability after climate. For a detailed summary of the relative importance of urbanization, climate, and regional setting indicators, see Fig. 6 and Table 3 and Supplementary Table 7. Future work should focus on improving model accuracy and enhancing applicability for robust cross-city UCD prediction.
